q1.question: What kind of garment photos should I upload for the best results?
q1.answer: Use clear photos where the garment is fully visible, not cropped, and not blocked by props or text. Flat-lay, ghost mannequin, or simple on-hanger shots work well. Avoid busy backgrounds, strong reflections, or heavy motion blur. If you are reusing existing model photos, make sure the clothing area is not already heavily retouched or distorted, otherwise the AI has less signal to work with.

q2.question: What are the main limitations of AI virtual try-on?
q2.answer: AI is very good at showing an overall look and feel, but it does not replace in-person fitting. Fine details like exact fabric thickness, stretch, or how a garment behaves across all body types may not be perfectly accurate. Extremely complex items (transparent fabrics, intricate lace, heavy reflections) are harder to generate reliably and may require more iterations or manual retouching afterwards.

q3.question: How do batch and catalog workflows work for large collections?
q3.answer: You can feed a list of SKUs into a batch workflow, attach one or more garment photos per SKU, and generate consistent model imagery in one run. The node graph stays the same – lighting, camera angle, background – while only the product inputs change. This is useful for refreshing an entire collection or unifying imagery across multiple seasons without rebuilding the pipeline every time.

q4.question: Can I control model diversity and brand representation?
q4.answer: Yes. You can specify desired attributes such as body type, skin tone, age range, and overall vibe (editorial, ecomm, streetwear) at the workflow or template level. Once you find a combination that matches your brand, you can reuse that setup across multiple shoots so your campaigns feel consistent and inclusive rather than random from batch to batch.

q5.question: Can I use AI fashion images in ads and marketplaces?
q5.answer: In many cases, yes – brands use AI model imagery for product pages, lookbooks, and paid campaigns. However, you are responsible for complying with local advertising rules, marketplace policies, and any disclosure requirements around AI-generated content. Avoid using real people's faces or logos without consent, and double-check that your platform allows AI-generated try-on images before scaling them.
